speaker: interviewer
text: Don't worry.

speaker: interviewer
text: You ready for me? Actually, you can slide it over the bars. [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: This is tape number five, with Anna Fischer on November 1, 1994, in Los Angeles, California. [PAUSES FOR 15 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Anytime.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. So you're on your way to Stuttgart, right?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, we are in Stuttgart already with the-- that bombardment in Stuttgart at night. And actually, when we-- when the-- this was a very heavy bombardment. We were maybe for three days under-- somewhere underground. They pulled us out maybe miles away. I thought we will never-- we will never get out of there.

speaker: interviewer
text: But-- but, you know, I always had the thought that-- because, you know, [INAUDIBLE] was, as far as I'm concerned, was the end of the Jewish people, I was thinking, look. If I'm going to die here, I will be a casualty of the war, like the German next to me, like anybody. I wasn't singled out because of what? Because of my big crime? That I-- that I-- I was born a Jew? So we were-- I suppose most of even the Poles were happy about bombardments. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: And when we got out the shelter, our room was, literally-- we lived on the third floor, I think. It was literally on the ground floor. If we'd stayed there maybe two or three minutes longer, we probably would've been dead. But again, whatever. And then-- so, for a short time-- I mean, but the Germans were so-- it's incredible how they-- see, they took it so-- it looked to me, so much in stride. They started cleaning up, started this, started that. And then the bombardment was still going on. And one of our-- this again, it's actually an aside. This isn't necessary.

speaker: interviewer
text: One of our Poles, [? Kaska ?], [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] her-- wasn't a husband. A boyfriend. Worked in something which was called boutzuk. You know-- you know what the boutzuk is? It's a train. It is a building train.

speaker: interviewee
text: And these were Poles working, so they were also slave laborers. When bridges were destroyed by the bombardment, the roads were destroyed by the bombardment, they were-- you know, and they were always parked in a tunnel. So they were always-- and-- and for a few nights after this big bombardment, we would go there to sleep for safety reasons. I think that there were two Jewish boys among them. I'm not sure. I mean, we never talked about it. But maybe, I don't know. Anyway, and then I was transferred, but, actually at my own request, to a TB sanatorium in Weinsberg, by Heilbronn. Because there were two Jewish girls-- I have them on the picture-- who were working there already. When they-- their places were bombed out, one was in [NON-ENGLISH], in the citizen home, and the other one  was some other restaurant-- anyway, they were transferred there. And this was such a bucolic, beautiful place. It was a pastoral environment. And they said, try to get here because it's so nice and quiet, no bombardment. It's 60 kilometres from Heilbronn. So I applied. And then they agreed. And so they were sorry to see me go because I'm such a nice person. You know, OK. And I was transferred there with another-- in fact, there was another Jewish girl who also knew already and who was bombed out. So she would transferred there too. So there we were five Jewish girls working together in this TB sanatorium [INAUDIBLE], until the end of the war. There was no bombardment. Heilbronn was bombarded. But then we were in different situations. But anyway, it was nothing of great--

speaker: interviewer
text: And no one ever had an idea. And did you ever talk amongst yourselves?

speaker: interviewee
text: Among-- among ourselves? We always talked about. That was another thing, that one of us-- she just died about two years ago. She had a beautiful voice. And she was always singing my Yiddish [INAUDIBLE] when we were working sometimes in the field. And we were always [SPEAKING YIDDISH]. Are you crazy? But-- but nobody-- nobody's-- those peasant women, they didn't really know very much. They just-- And-- and the doctors, the head doctors, Dr. [? Hauser, ?] He thought-- I don't know-- he knew that we are not-- so he thought that we may be in the Polish underground. That we are-- somehow, we are not fitting in. And-- and he was-- he was very-- but-- but he never said anything. Well, he was an anti-Nazi. You know, when they would be all, Heil Hitler, he never would say Heil Hitler. He always said [SPEAKING GERMAN] which means, you know, 'good afternoon,' or 'good  morning.'

speaker: interviewer
text: So and-- and there it was lovely. Only maybe the last night before the Americans came in, there was quite a bit of shooting. And-- and then we got up in the morning, and the Americans were there. So we idiots thought that they would immediately know that we are somebody that they shouldn't shoot at. So we were walking out. And-- and then the big tank came. And somebody took out a machine gun. And he said, I don't know, probably in English, which we didn't understand-- if you don't turn back, I'm going to shoot. They didn't know-- [INAUDIBLE] especially, she thought they would take a look at her, and they would greet her with-- well, so that's how-- So how did you come to realize-- what happened at that moment? If the Americans were there, how did they come to understand you were--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the-- the first-- the first thing which we did was, the next day, we didn't go to work. And the nun, the one sister so and so, she came up, and she says, why are you not going to work? And we tell her-- we told the Sister Emma [SPEAKING GERMAN] You understand? Well, you know, you never heard that the war is over for us? And-- and those Americans, you know, they were really unprepared for all those slave laborers, and they didn't know what to do. There were a lot of them. You know, there were Poles who were working in the fields there, and we knew-- some of them were our best friends. [CHUCKLES] And so we-- we just-- maybe for a week or two, we were just sitting there, going down, taking food, coming back up. And-- and-- we-- we didn't know anything. We didn't know. For all we knew at that time, we might have been the only three Jewish-- five Jewish people left. I mean, we-- we were totally cut off. It's really, it's funny when you think of it now.

speaker: interviewer
text: At this point, did anyone know you were Jewish, or you still hid your identity?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nobody-- nobody in Weinsberg, nobody ever-- nobody ever knew. And then, what happened, the Americans, there was some, I mean, captain, who, he took over, and they started organizing. Because there were nationalities. There were Poles. There were Ukrainians. There were Italians. There were French. There were-- I mean, all the nationalities as slave laborers.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they found themselves with so many people. And there was a-- there were big barracks. There was a base where the Germans kept POWs. I think American-- not American. English and French POWs. But when-- before the Americans came, they shipped them all out. So those barracks were empty. So the Americans, maybe say, maybe a week, maybe weeks later, started filling up those barracks with the displaced persons and-- and started sending them home, repatriating them. Those French went home first. The Belgians went home second. Anyway. And then we were left. We were the Poles. So we-- and-- and at that time, they still did not know. The Poles, our good friends. So we decided-- you know, we felt-- I mean, we really felt, in a way, you know, sort of freer. And so we decided we have to go to this American and tell him who we are and that we really do not want to remain here. That we want to go to Stuttgart to find out if there are any other Jews left.

speaker: interviewee
text: And this fellow, we hit upon a Texan, who had no idea, I think, about the Holocaust, who was sitting and strumming. I'll never forget-- the guitar singing [INAUDIBLE], which we'd never heard, but now I know this by-- music by heart. [CHUCKLES] Melody by heart. And when we finally tried to explain to him why we wanted to go to Stuttgart, so he says, well, you know, there is no transportation left for anybody. You know, it's only the-- war [INAUDIBLE] ended. And as soon as I can help you with something-- and at that time, he became sort of our friend. And every morning, he called me in and gave us a small piece of Hershey bars to divide in five to five. You know, we thought it was something terrific.

speaker: interviewer
text: Anyway, so at that moment, we decided we are going to tell our friends who we are. And-- and we did. They asked us why did we go in? You just don't go into American officer, especially since you don't speak the language. Anyway, when we told them who we are, they, maybe 15 minutes later, sent a delegation to the poor fellow that didn't know what they were talking about to remove us from the Polish barracks because they don't want to be together with Jews. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So we still couldn't travel, until, I don't know, about another week or two weeks. No, I think, no, no. I think Rabbi Eskin, then I think a couple of Jews came to our-- no. Yeah, this was still in Weinsberg. There were some Italian boys who became our good friends. And they were giving performances. And they knew that-- we told them that we are Jewish. But they didn't have any objections to-- to that.

speaker: interviewee
text: And there were-- and then I think there were a couple of-- a few Jewish inmates started coming out and-- and still got into Weinsberg. And then, [SIGHS] it was something, I know, that Marina and I, we were arrested by the Americans because we wanted to escape from this-- yeah, then they announced that we have to stay in this camp until, you know, they will dispose, one way or the other, with us. And we, because we still had our room in the TB sanatorium, and our things, so we decided, well, we are going to steal out, the two of us, Marina and I, and go. And then-- then we'll come back when things straighten out, whatever. So we walked out. And we started walking down the road. And before we knew, a jeep came, picked us up, took us back to the camp, and closed us in. They put me in one room and Marina in the second room. We are arrested. So we were never arrested by the Germans. We were arrested for trying to escape. But this, I mean, somebody else came. You know, and we-- and so they-- they let us out. And-- and then-- Rabbi-- yeah, Rabbi Eskin came, I think, already to Weinsberg. Because a few more Jews came. And he was a chaplain. And he hit upon, you know, some Jewish former inmates in Weinsberg. So he was actually the one that took us to Stuttgart and opened this first displaced persons camp, which was a beautiful villa in [INAUDIBLE]. [INAUDIBLE]. And more and more Jews were coming out. And we were there. Well, we didn't do much. But we didn't know, really, what was happening. And at that time-- this is another thing, the American GIs. You know. And they were getting all kinds of-- the-- the American Jews woke up to the fact what was happening. They were such young kids. They were 18, 19 years old, the occupation forces.

speaker: interviewer
text: And they would come to us in the DP camp and ask us, you know, for the names. Do we have any relatives in America. That's how-- that's how I am in Los Angeles. Do we have any relatives in America? Do you know the name? Maybe they can, you know, they will send it to their families because their families asked them. So I knew that my mother had an uncle in Los Angeles. This was-- my mother's maiden name was Bauman. This was family Bauman. They were-- five sons and two daughters. And one of them even visited us in 1935 with her husband. I remember her, Julia and Morris.

speaker: interviewee
text: And so I was just giving out this. But I knew the name. I didn't know the-- I knew it's Los Angeles. I didn't know the street. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] But so-- so I don't know how many, you know, you were just giving it to every Jewish fellow.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then, of course, some of them were those young kids. And what they were ready to-- you know, they were getting those, what do call those rations, the D-rations? The soldiers, what are they getting, this packet? C-rations.

speaker: interviewee
text: One of-- some of them made a business out of selling those C-rations and cigarettes. You know, they were getting four cigarettes, I think, in their little packet. And then, of course, Lucky Strike, some of them really made a fortune, I think, selling cartons of cigarettes. I suppose those that were working-- some of them would bring to the DP camp a big can of pineapple. And we just saw this as an absolutely-- you know, pineapple was very, very expensive in Poland. You ate it, maybe, for Purim or for Rosh Hashanah. You sliced. You bought a half a pineapple. You sliced it very, very thin. You didn't peel it because you would lose some of it. And here, we saw those cans of pineapple. We thought these Americans, they are all millionaires. And this white bread. Then we found out that it didn't-- but-- but to us, it was the-- it-- we-- we thought that it is-- even if it didn't taste, but we thought it just was such white bread. It was neither here nor there. And so I wanted to-- and then, in the meanwhile, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I mean, when Rabbi Eskin, when we came to-- to Stuttgart, and opened this DP camp, which actually was a small beautiful villa in [INAUDIBLE], because there were very few of us at the time. And-- and a-- a short time later, the Joint Distribution Committee opened an office. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And one day I received a letter from the Joint, that-- somebody communicated with me on the Joint-- that actually it was, you know, some people, I suppose some people are businesspeople. And some, even among our girls, and some went into business. I mean, they were buying cigarettes. They were selling cigarettes. They were doing this. They were doing that. You know, I was always-- but I was trying to find a job to do something until, whatever, you know, things will be disposed of. And I spoke to one of the-- he was a nice fellow, I remember. It was an officer. And that-- I mean, it was-- some officer. Kurtz was his name, who, because they opened an American-- what do you call, military service, military offices, we used to call them.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I applied for a job. And I got the job, granted, maybe for two or three months. I was supposed to be-- and in the meanwhile, I started taking English lessons. And I spoke-- I really spoke perfect German at that time-- that I should be a special investigator-- it sounded great-- for public health issues.

speaker: interviewee
text: Which meant that I should-- this was a field job-- to go to-- well, you had-- you had pharmacies. You didn't have drug stores, you know. To-- to go to the pharmacies, and go to the dental depots, and make sure that the Nazi owners were removed because they were putting in substitutes. Americans. They were-- they were trying. The Americans didn't know what they were doing. They were no occupation forces. They were never doing it. And-- but-- but for me, I-- I got the [? badge. ?] Maybe I still have this ticket, I don't know. And it occupied my time. I was getting lunch there. And I was paid some, I don't remember how many marks. And then, I-- when the Joint Distribution Office opened, by then, there were more Jews, and the big-- you know, the big DP camps where [INAUDIBLE]. There were hundreds and hundreds coming. So I went to the Joint, and I offered my services. I mean, voluntary services. People needed, you know, translations from Yiddish to Polish, from Yiddish to German, from Polish to German, from German to Polish, God knows.

speaker: interviewer
text: So after the war, I usually, you know, went in there and became quite friendly with the woman who was running it. Her name was-- her name was Flor Levine. And one day, she received-- the Joint Distribution received a letter.

speaker: interviewee
text: I looked at this letter the other day from my mother's cousin. These ones, they are all dead-- that she was overjoyed to hear that-- well, nobody knew me. I mean, they didn't know who I am. But I always was saying, you know, that my mother's-- one of my mother's children remained alive. That they were overjoyed to hear, that they would try to bring me to America as soon as-- as they can. And this, I don't know if it's true or not. I mean, this Flor Levine, she was telling me that, it's a lovely family. Well, lovely I expected them to be, but you know, that they are-- she knows them, and if I need anything, she will advance some money for me because they were-- well, I didn't know any--

speaker: interviewer
text: By this time, had you and Beno come together, or what happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, by this time, we did not. And-- and then she also promised me, she said, well, you know, pretty soon-- we don't know how soon, but it took quite a few months-- the American consulate will open in Stuttgart. And as soon as it opens, you will be the first-- one of the first one on the list to get a visa. See, the point was that you couldn't come here. You couldn't get-- I mean, you still needed affidavit. And affidavit were still from before the war because Americans didn't want, you know, immigrants. And it's only Truman in 1947 or 1948, I think, where finally that law passed through the Congress, you know, the special offer immigration of-- of-- for-- for displaced persons, for refugees, the refugees law. And we actually did arrive under-- because my cousins wrote to them, and they said they will be glad to provide an affidavit for me that I won't become a burden to the state. And that was actually the main thing.

speaker: interviewer
text: So and then, I mean, Beno didn't tell you how we met? Yes, he did.

speaker: interviewee
text: So you know. So one day, well, you know, you were always looking for lists of maybe somebody's-- I was not only looking for his name. I was looking for the names of my brothers, my sisters, and nobody remained. And one day, this was May 27-- March 27, 1948.

speaker: interviewer
text: So Beno told you. He went to-- to Munich because-- and then he met Felix Alund, whose mother survived the war. And so Beno was going to come to Stuttgart to say hello to her because he was with his father together, you know, in Plassenburg, you know all this. So and-- and-- and my friends, of course, we lived in [NON-ENGLISH], the five of us. And of course, they knew everything about [INAUDIBLE], you know. Each one of us, you know, had somebody to talk-- to tell you about it. It didn't exist anymore, we thought. And that day, I went, after my work, that I was-- always had a briefcase.

speaker: interviewee
text: I mean, I was presentable. I pretended to be a somebody. And after my work, I went down to the Joint Distribution Committee. I had some-- I mean, Flor, she wanted me to find whatever papers to fill out. And I came there, down there.

speaker: interviewer
text: And, well, again, you know, because I was so-called-- I mean, connected with it. All these people standing around me asking me, what is? And I don't know if Beno told you that I had-- we had tickets with some girlfriends. We had tickets to a concert that evening. Did Beno say?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, you know, [INAUDIBLE] there was supposed be a flute solo concerto. And one of friends bought some tickets for us. A group of a few friends. And so I was going down. And then-- and then I was-- was standing and waiting to-- for the bus, Stuttgart station, actually, to go back to [INAUDIBLE] to have dinner. And then we were going to go to this concert, I think, at 8 o'clock. I probably still have the program.

speaker: interviewer
text: And so people were sitting around me. And I was, you know, talking. And I suddenly felt as if somebody was-- was sort of-- you know, as if somebody is looking, as if you had eyes in the back. And I turned around, and I said, Beno, you are alive? And that's how we met again. And he had ticket-- a ticket for the same concert. So it sounds idyllic. And what happened? You got married?

speaker: interviewee
text: We got-- we got married so-- and then, in the meanwhile, you know, by then, I already got this affidavit. And the-- the-- and-- [SIGHS] with-- we are supposed to go with the-- I was supposed to go with the first. I already went through the consulate, and I was supposed to go with the first boat. [INAUDIBLE], it was a military boat. And I did. But then I came to Flor, and I told, you know, I found my boyfriend, and I would like him to. And first she said, you're making a mistake. You know. You don't know survivor, what and how and you may-- in America, you may meet a rich-- meet-- meet a rich millionaire, of course, waiting for me. You know.

speaker: interviewer
text: So but at the time, when you're young, and you are in love, and, I mean, and we met in such a way again. I mean, it really sounded miraculous. So she said, OK, I will get an affidavit for him too so you will go together. So when did you get married?

speaker: interviewee
text: We got married in April in Stuttgart. April 24, and left April 28 and came to America [CHUCKLES] May 20, 1946. And my cousins were couple-- that couple was waiting for me. They were shocked when they saw that I'm coming down with a man.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how many children do you have now?

speaker: interviewee
text: Three.

speaker: interviewer
text: And can you tell us about your children and you grandchildren?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, well our oldest daughter is Tessa. And she is-- you know, we are very proverbial-- what they call? Occupations. And she's a doctor. She's a physician. She's has a family practice and also geriatrics. And she has been-- she never had her own office. She was always working with a group. And she lives in Evanston, Chicago. In Evanston, Illinois. [CHUCKLES] You know, it's so--

speaker: interviewer
text: And how many children does she have?

speaker: interviewee
text: She has two children. Lia, who is-- who is going to be 16 in February, 7-- 11. Tessa's birthday is on February 7. And Gabe, Gabriel, Gabe, who is going to be Bar Mitzvah now. Is it 19th of-- 19th of November. So we are going to Chicago for [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: And you have what? Another girl, or?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I have another girl, Karen, who is an over-educated person. She-- [CHUCKLES] she was in AFSA. She speaks fluently Danish. She came back here. She graduated from, got a-- a bachelors at UCLA with cumma sum laude. Whatever, she's always on the top. And then she didn't know what to do with herself. And everybody was telling her, you know, she is such a brain, she should go and apply to a law school. And she-- you know, for the lack of anything else, she applied to all of the six-- I think this is something interesting-- to all the California law schools.

speaker: interviewer
text: Everybody was telling her, why don't you apply to Harvard? Why don't they-- and she said, my parents don't have this kind of money. California school will be enough. And she was really accepted to all, to-- to BOLT, to UCLA, to Davis, to I mean, to all of them, whatever there is left, Hasting, everything. And so she went to BOLT and got a law degree and didn't like law. She felt it is so-- it's is altogether different than what you really expect it to be.

speaker: interviewee
text: But since she was a very good student, so she, anyway, she will take the bar the first time around, when everything is, you know, fresh in her mind. And she took the bar, and she quickly passed it and worked for a law-- for some friends who wanted to hook her up with some big firms here. But she said, I don't think that I would be interested in big corporation. She was not interested. All she still-- I mean, our kids are idealistic. We never pushed them, and we never made any money either. Anyway, so she worked for one year on criminal-- criminal-- on criminal law and-- and hated it.

speaker: interviewer
text: And she's married now?

speaker: interviewee
text: She's-- she's married now. She married only recently, she-- she went five years ago.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where does she live?

speaker: interviewee
text: She lives in San Francisco. And what-- then what she did, so she did not want to do the law anymore. So we talked about it. And anyway, she was very much interested in performing arts, especially in ballet.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we had a friend here who, actually, he was originator of that-- maybe you know that it is Performing Arts Administration, it's part of the MBA. And I said, well, if Karen would like to, you know, do UCLA, she could get that degree if she wants to-- even, you know, to put together, to connect law and these things. So she came down back to California. And she she got her degree in performing arts administration. She worked for four years at the Met in New York, always with this administrative thing, and then at Joffrey for five years. And then when Joffrey died, and things, you know, she wasn't happy there anymore, she-- she got the job with-- then she met John, and they got married. And she is now with the Cal Performances at Berkeley. Do you know what Cal Performance is? It's like [INAUDIBLE]. You know, UCLA. So and she's quite a-- but she's expecting a baby now in January. So I hope-- she's not going to be a very young mother, but I hope everything will be OK. And you have a third child?

speaker: interviewee
text: And then I have a son, David, who is a graphic designer, who didn't want to go into architecture. He's a nice kid. I think he's-- he's probably the nicest person. He lives with this French-- I mean, Jewish girl, but a French girl, now for almost 16 years.

speaker: interviewer
text: It was also our mistake, probably. After he graduated from UCLA, we sent him on a trip to-- you know, we had some-- Beno had some relatives in Paris. He probably told you. And of course, my relatives in Israel. But the kids were with us the first time around in Europe, you know, 1963. Did Beno show you that picture? No? No, but-- but we're finishing with this tape.

speaker: interviewee
text: Maybe that's enough.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Is this tape finished? Why don't we--
